---
title: "Showcase"
subtitle: "A tour of what Quarto can do"
toc: true
---

This page is a grab-bag of Quarto features, rendered right in your site. Open the `.qmd` source alongside this page to see how each piece is written.

## Callouts

Callouts are a nice way to flag notes, tips, warnings, or important info without writing any HTML.

::: {.callout-note}
This is a **note**. Good for asides and extra context.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro tip
Callouts can have their own titles, like this one.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
This is a **warning**. Use it to flag things readers should be careful about.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
This is **important**. Reserve it for things that really matter.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Click to expand
Callouts can also be collapsible — handy for optional detail or spoilers.
:::

## Tabsets

Tabsets let you show alternative content (e.g. different languages or OS instructions) without cluttering the page.

::: {.panel-tabset}
## macOS
```bash
brew install quarto
```

## Windows
```powershell
winget install --id Posit.Quarto
```

## Linux
```bash
sudo apt install quarto
```
:::

## Columns / layout

Quarto uses a grid system so you can lay content out side by side.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Left column
Put anything here — text, images, code.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Right column
This is great for before/after comparisons or a figure next to its explanation.
:::
:::

## Code blocks

Plain fenced code blocks get syntax highlighting for free, with optional line numbers:

```python
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("world"))
```

You can also fold long code blocks so they don't dominate the page — click to expand:

```{.python code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the full script"}
import math

def area_of_circle(radius: float) -> float:
    return math.pi * radius ** 2

for r in range(1, 5):
    print(f"radius={r} -> area={area_of_circle(r):.2f}")
```

## Live, interactive demo (Observable JS)

Quarto ships with [Observable JS](https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/) built in — no Python/R kernel required, it runs straight in the browser. Drag the slider:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof n = Inputs.range([1, 50], {value: 10, step: 1, label: "n"})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
md`**${n} squared is ${n * n}.**`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  marks: [
    Plot.barY(
      Array.from({length: n}, (_, i) => ({x: i + 1, y: (i + 1) ** 2})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "steelblue"}
    )
  ],
  y: {label: "value²"},
  x: {label: "value"}
})
```

## Tables

| Feature | Needs a kernel? | Good for |
|---|---|---|
| Callouts | No | Notes, warnings, tips |
| Tabsets | No | Alternatives (OS, language) |
| Observable JS | No | Live interactive charts/widgets |
| Python/R code chunks | Yes (Jupyter/knitr) | Data analysis, real computation |

## Footnotes & cross-references

You can drop a footnote right in the text.[^1] Figures and tables can also be numbered and cross-referenced automatically once you add captions and labels.

[^1]: Like this one — Quarto collects them at the bottom of the page automatically.

## Theme toggle

Look at the navbar — there's now a light/dark switcher (configured in `_quarto.yml` via `theme: {light: cosmo, dark: darkly}`). Try it.

## Where to go next

- [Callouts](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/callouts.html)
- [Tabsets](https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/layout.html#tabset-panel)
- [Observable JS](https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/)
- [Themes](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-themes.html)
- [Full website guide](https://quarto.org/docs/websites)
